turns.text: Good question. The answer depends on how soon you need the money. Here's a quick explainer.

data.headline: Funds or savings? It depends on time
data.body: If you need the money within 18 months, a savings deposit protects your capital — no market risk. If your horizon is longer, a fund can grow more, but the value moves up and down along the way.
data.checkQuestion: Emma's trip is about 14 months away. What fits best?
data.trustNote: Based on your 14-month timeline for the Japan trip.
data.noraNote: Under 18 months means protecting what you save matters more than chasing growth.

turns.text: Is putting money in funds actually safe? I've heard people lose everything
turns.pickedChip: Is putting money in funds safe?

turns.text: That's a fair concern — and a common one. I drafted a short guide on how fund risk actually works over time. Worth a 3-minute read.

resource.title: Can you actually lose everything in a fund?
resource.summary: A calm look at what fund risk means in practice, why time is your biggest safety net, and what history shows about 3-year horizons.
resource.body:
When people say they lost everything in the market, they usually mean they sold during a crash. A broadly diversified index fund has never gone to zero. Individual stocks can collapse, but a fund that holds hundreds of companies spreads that risk across the whole market.

The key factor is time. Over any single month, a stock fund might drop 5–10%. Over a year, drops of 20% have happened. But over 3 years or more, the historical odds shift strongly in your favour. Since 1990, a global index fund held for any rolling 3-year period has been positive about 90% of the time.

This is why Nora matches the product to your timeline. Under 18 months, a savings deposit protects your capital with zero market risk. Between 18 months and 4 years, a balanced mix (savings plus some funds) gives you growth potential while keeping most of the money safe. Over 4 years, equity funds make sense because you have time to ride out the dips.

The real risk is not investing at all. Inflation quietly eats savings that sit in a zero-interest account. A 3-year timeline like yours is long enough to benefit from a balanced approach, while short enough that you would not put everything in equities.

No one can guarantee returns. But understanding how risk and time interact means you can make a calm, informed choice instead of avoiding funds out of fear.
keyTakeaways: Diversified index funds have never gone to zero — individual stocks have
A 3-year horizon puts historical odds strongly in your favour
The biggest risk for long-term savings is often doing nothing (inflation)
Nora matches the product mix to your timeline automatically
